title: ENTER/EXIT_PLAN_MODE_MARKER protocol uses string markers via super:: instead of typed control plane
description:
## Severity
Minor

## Dimension
Decoupling / Architectural Alignment

## Location
src/tools/plan_mode.rs:4-13 (imports + execute_enter_plan_mode) and 17-39 (execute_exit_plan_mode)

## Violation
```rust
use super::{ENTER_PLAN_MODE_MARKER, EXIT_PLAN_MODE_MARKER};
...
let result = json!({"type": ENTER_PLAN_MODE_MARKER});
(result.to_string(), false)
```

The tool returns a JSON-serialized object containing a magic string marker that the calling main loop must parse to know "the model entered plan mode." This is in-band signalling via the tool result channel that was designed for "return text to the model." The dispatcher must:
1. String-parse every tool result.
2. Check if it parses as JSON.
3. Check if the JSON has a `type` field matching one of two magic strings.
4. If so, intercept and route to a control-flow handler instead of forwarding to the model.

This is the Big Ball of Mud in miniature: tool results carry side-channel control commands. Every other tool's result text is data; these two tools' result texts are control messages. The naming (returning "(String, bool)" but actually meaning "(ControlMessage | UserText, isError)") is misleading.

## Mandated refactor
Replace `(String, bool)` return with an enum:
```rust
pub enum ToolResult {
    Text { content: String, is_error: bool },
    EnterPlanMode,
    ExitPlanMode { allowed_prompts: Vec<AllowedPrompt> },
    ...
}
```
The dispatcher matches the enum explicitly; the model never sees the marker strings. This makes the control plane and data plane separate — the architectural boundary the markers currently violate. Cross-references issue #670.

## Filed from QA review dispatch
